title: Do You Need To Massage Dermal Filler？ | Dr Tim's Tips
filename: Do You Need To Massage Dermal Filler？ ｜ Dr Tim_s Tips.en.vtt
excerpt: So how do you massage dermal fillers after a procedure well the first thing is you don't necessarily have to massage them for most types of dermal filler if it feels ok on the day and you can't see any lumps or bumps you can leave it alone even small imperfections will tend to in…

segments.text: so how do you massage dermal fillers after a procedure well the first thing is you don't necessarily have to massage them for most types of dermal filler if it feels ok on the day and you can't see any lumps or bumps you can leave it alone even small imperfections will tend to integrate within the first two or three weeks so you needn't massage them at all if you do decide to massage them it's important to know a little bit about the anatomy so that you don't make problems worse as a general rule we want to get rid of lumps or bumps but not try and change the decision of dermal fillers so comic complaint would be a lump in a lip for example if you feel a lump in your lip it's better to start with a small amount of pressure gradually increase the pressure until you feel the lump ease underneath your fingers and then stop it's all about smoothing it not moving it this is what I say to my trainees and there's what you should do for your clients as well take your fingers together massage gently increasing the pressure until you get the movement that you want and it gradually eases there's no lump or

bump if the area is something else so for example sometimes people get a dima after a procedure it looks a bit like dermal filler but it's actually just puffiness that is not going to respond to massaging and you probably shouldn't massage it at all you should just wait be particularly careful about massaging dermal filler into areas that will make the aesthetic result worse so for example if you massage your cheek treatment just after the procedure I think this is quite hard to do if it's happening if it's months ago but say you had a little bit of dermal filler here and you massaged it in the wrong direction you could get a bit of puffiness underneath the eye so as a general rule you want to massage away from the eyes away from the lips or hold it if you're trying to preserve volume you massage in a way that acknowledges the shape that you're initially trying to create instead of flattening its and losing the aesthetic result that you were after so it's not particularly difficult there's actually very rare that you'll cause a problem by massaging probably the biggest risk is that you'll just slightly soften the result that might have been good so wherever possible give your clients good advice on doing this to the bare minimum

and when you're going to do them massaging your results for your clients make sure that you do it in a way that acknowledges the result you initially were trying to achieve rather than just generally just massaging and the aesthetics that's probably the biggest mistake clinicians make
